label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking why some groups support the new law in Virginia regarding the ignition interlock devices for first-time drunk driving offenders. First, I need to recall the information from the passage. The new law requires more people to install these devices, which prevent the car from starting if the driver's blood alcohol is over the limit. The groups supporting it include Mothers Against Drunk Driving and the Washington Regional Alcohol Program. They mention that Virginia had 274 alcohol-related deaths and over 5,500 injuries in 2010, which they consider too high. They also say that these devices reduce repeat offenses. Looking at the options: A) The government can be financed to build roads – Hmm, the passage doesn't mention anything about road construction funding. The focus is on reducing drunk driving incidents. So probably not A. B) Some traffic deaths and injuries may be avoided – This aligns with the groups' argument that the current numbers are unacceptably high and that the devices help reduce repeat offenses, which would in turn prevent accidents. So B seems possible. C) Lower-income drivers will not afford to drink again – The passage actually mentions that some opponents argue lower-income drivers will be affected by the fees. The supporters are talking about reducing accidents, not about economic impact on drinking. So C doesn't seem right. D) The court system is forced to work more effectively – The passage says some lawyers think the court system will
